Musical artist

**James Edward Gadson** (born June 17, 1939) is an American [drummer](/wiki/Drummer "Drummer") and [session musician](/wiki/Session_musician "Session musician"). Beginning his career in the late 1960s, Gadson has since become one of the most-recorded drummers in the history of [R&B](/wiki/Rhythm_and_blues "Rhythm and blues").[[1]](#cite_note-1) He is also a singer and songwriter.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=James_Gadson&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Career")]

Born in [Kansas City, Missouri](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), Gadson played with the first line-up of Charles Wright's [Watts 103rd Street Rhythm Band](/wiki/Charles_Wright_%26_the_Watts_103rd_Street_Rhythm_Band "Charles Wright & the Watts 103rd Street Rhythm Band"),[[3]](#cite_note-3) and recorded three albums with them between 1968 and 1970. Along with other members of Wright's band, he went on to appear on many hit records, including with [Dyke & the Blazers](/wiki/Dyke_and_the_Blazers "Dyke and the Blazers").[[4]](#cite_note-4) Gadson started to become well known as a drummer following the release of the album *[Still Bill](/wiki/Still_Bill "Still Bill")* by [Bill Withers](/wiki/Bill_Withers "Bill Withers"),[[5]](#cite_note-5) released by [Sussex Records](/wiki/Sussex_Records "Sussex Records") in 1972. He played on [The Temptations](/wiki/The_Temptations "The Temptations") album *[1990](/wiki/1990_(The_Temptations_album) "1990 (The Temptations album)")*,[[6]](#cite_note-6) released on the [Motown](/wiki/Motown_Records "Motown Records") label in 1973.

In 1975, he played with [Freddie King](/wiki/Freddie_King "Freddie King") on *Larger Than Life*[[7]](#cite_note-7) and went on to record with [Martha Reeves](/wiki/Martha_Reeves "Martha Reeves"), [Randy Crawford](/wiki/Randy_Crawford "Randy Crawford"), [Quincy Jones](/wiki/Quincy_Jones "Quincy Jones"), [Herbie Hancock](/wiki/Herbie_Hancock "Herbie Hancock"), [B.B. King](/wiki/B.B._King "B.B. King"), [Albert King](/wiki/Albert_King "Albert King"), [Rose Royce](/wiki/Rose_Royce "Rose Royce"), [Elkie Brooks](/wiki/Elkie_Brooks "Elkie Brooks") and many more artists.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 1975, he anchored the Motown classic double platinum album [*City Of Angels*](/wiki/City_of_Angels_(The_Miracles_album) "City of Angels (The Miracles album)"), recorded by [Billy Griffin & The Miracles](/wiki/The_Miracles "The Miracles").

Gadson was also the drummer on Marvin Gaye's "I Want You" in 1976 and [Diana Ross](/wiki/Diana_Ross "Diana Ross")'s hit 1976 single [Love Hangover](/wiki/Love_Hangover "Love Hangover"). He drummed on [Thelma Houston](/wiki/Thelma_Houston "Thelma Houston")'s breakout disco hit, "[Don't Leave Me This Way](/wiki/Don%27t_Leave_Me_This_Way "Don't Leave Me This Way")" (1976), recorded at Motown's [West Hollywood](/wiki/West_Hollywood "West Hollywood") studio complex.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

He appeared on two tracks, "At The Mercy" and "Riding To Vanity Fair", on the 2005 [Paul McCartney](/wiki/Paul_McCartney "Paul McCartney") album *[Chaos and Creation in the Backyard](/wiki/Chaos_and_Creation_in_the_Backyard "Chaos and Creation in the Backyard")*.

He has a brief appearance in the [Adam Sandler](/wiki/Adam_Sandler "Adam Sandler") 2009 movie *[Funny People](/wiki/Funny_People "Funny People")* as a member of the jam band that Sandler's character hires to play with him.

In April 2009, Gadson joined Alex Dixon, grandson of [Willie Dixon](/wiki/Willie_Dixon "Willie Dixon"), on his 2009 release titled *Rising From The Bushes,* in which he appeared on two tracks, "Fantasy" and Willie Dixon's famous song "[Spoonful](/wiki/Spoonful "Spoonful")".[[10]](#cite_note-10)

In June 2009, Gadson joined [Beck](/wiki/Beck "Beck"), [Wilco](/wiki/Wilco "Wilco"), [Feist](/wiki/Feist_(singer) "Feist (singer)") and [Jamie Lidell](/wiki/Jamie_Lidell "Jamie Lidell") covering [Skip Spence](/wiki/Skip_Spence "Skip Spence")'s *[Oar](/wiki/Oar_(album) "Oar (album)")* as part of Beck's Record Club series, with videos appearing on Beck's website beginning November 2009.[[11]](#cite_note-11) He has drummed on Beck's albums *[Sea Change](/wiki/Sea_Change_(album) "Sea Change (album)")*, *[The Information](/wiki/The_Information_(Beck_album) "The Information (Beck album)")* and *[Morning Phase](/wiki/Morning_Phase "Morning Phase")*, as well as [Jamie Lidell](/wiki/Jamie_Lidell "Jamie Lidell")'s 2010 album *[Compass](/wiki/Compass_(Jamie_Lidell_album) "Compass (Jamie Lidell album)")*. Gadson played drums, as well as hambone (slapping his legs), on the [D'Angelo](/wiki/D%27Angelo "D'Angelo") song "Sugah Daddy", on the *[Black Messiah](/wiki/Black_Messiah_(album) "Black Messiah (album)")* album (2014).*[[12]](#cite_note-12)* He appeared in the 2016 video for “Mama Can’t Help You No More,” by [Doyle Bramhall II](/wiki/Doyle_Bramhall_II "Doyle Bramhall II").

In 2019, James Gadson, who resides in Los Angeles, was featured on *[Gordon Ramsay's 24 Hours to Hell and Back](/wiki/Gordon_Ramsay%27s_24_Hours_to_Hell_and_Back "Gordon Ramsay's 24 Hours to Hell and Back")* as his paternal niece's and nephew-in-law's restaurant, Bayou on the Vine, was renamed "Gadson's Restaurant & Jazz Club", named after him and his late brother, guitarist Thomas Maurice 'Tutty' Gadson (died 2014).[[13]](#cite_note-13)
